refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning sensor acquisition, network transmission, and command execution.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency from operator input (e.g., joystick command) through network to equipment response (e.g., actuator movement) under representative network conditions. Collect statistical distribution and confirm p95 ≤ 150 ms across 1000+ samples under load.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission (uplink and downlink) shall introduce ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Remote command processing and actuator response shall introduce ≤ 40 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations to operator and safety systems

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement edge computing with local sensor fusion and predictive buffering; use low-latency network protocol (UDP-based) with adaptive compression; employ hardware-accelerated command execution on vehicle controller
DP-B: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with optimized network routing and QoS prioritization; implement client-side prediction and dead-reckoning for latency masking

DP-C: Hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback; edge processing for critical sensor streams; network-agnostic command protocol with redundant paths; latency monitoring and adaptive mode switching
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback; edge processing for critical sensor streams; network-agnostic command protocol with redundant paths; latency monitoring and adaptive mode switching

potential_sources: Coupling between latency requirement and safety criticality: DP-C's fallback autonomy directly couples to safety services (SafetyAlert interface)
Coupling between network protocol choice and teleoperation module design: DP-C requires tight integration with Network Link interface
Coupling between latency monitoring and operator feedback: DP-C requires integration with Tasking interface for mode switching signals
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred because it decouples latency performance from network reliability through local autonomy, reducing single-point-of-failure risk. Couplings are manageable through well-defined interfaces (Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute capacity for edge processing and local autonomy fallback
Assumption: Network conditions are measurable and predictable within agreed bounds
Assumption: Operator can safely manage mode transitions (tele-op to autonomous fallback)
Risk: If edge compute is insufficient, latency targets may not be achievable; mitigation: early hardware validation
Risk: Network variability exceeds agreed bounds; mitigation: design fallback autonomy to be safe under worst-case latency
Risk: Operator confusion during mode transitions; mitigation: clear HMI feedback and training
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) latency budget allocation across sensor, network, and actuator paths; (2) network protocol selection criteria (UDP vs. TCP trade-offs); (3) fallback autonomy trigger thresholds; (4) latency monitoring and reporting mechanism.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses remote operator interface, command encoding/decoding, network protocol management, latency monitoring, and coordination with Vehicle for command execution and with Safety Services for safety-critical alerts. Does NOT own physical network infrastructure (Network Link) or vehicle actuators (Vehicle).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel for operator commands (uplink) and telemetry/status (downlink). Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for latency performance; must monitor and adapt to network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends decoded operator commands (e.g., movement, tool control) to Vehicle via Tasking interface. Latency of this interface is critical to end-to-end performance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives sensor data and vehicle state from Vehicle via Telemetry interface. Used for operator feedback and latency monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency violations, mode transitions, and safety-critical events to Safety Services. Enables safety system to intervene if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency performance. All latency-critical logic (command encoding, network protocol, monitoring, fallback coordination) belongs here. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert reporting and Tasking interface coordination required.

Vehicle owns actuator response latency (part of DP-C) but not network or operator-side latency. If latency requirement assigned to Vehicle, would require Vehicle to own network protocol and operator interface logic—inappropriate diffusion.
Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, not latency performance itself. Assigning latency requirement here would diffuse teleoperation logic across modules.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Assigning latency requirement here would not clarify ownership or enable focused design; latency is a Teleoperation-specific concern.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner because: (1) it directly controls operator-side latency (command encoding, HMI responsiveness); (2) it manages network protocol and latency monitoring; (3) it coordinates with Vehicle and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces; (4) it can implement DP-C (hybrid architecture with fallback) without diffusing logic across modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become too large if it also owns fallback autonomy logic. Mitigation: fallback autonomy is owned by Vehicle; Teleoperation only triggers it via Tasking interface.
Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient latency visibility. Mitigation: Teleoperation must instrument Network Link to measure and report latency metrics.
Risk: SafetyAlert interface may not support real-time latency reporting. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert protocol to include latency violation messages with priority levels.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify that Teleoperation owns latency performance for the tele-op path (operator → network → vehicle command execution). Vehicle owns actuator response latency. Safety Services owns latency-triggered safety interventions. Define explicit latency budget allocation across these modules in the DP.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback, edge processing, network-agnostic protocol, redundant paths, latency monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). DP-C's fallback autonomy and adaptive mode switching provide safety margins if network latency exceeds thresholds. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: DP-C requires coordination between Teleoperation (command encoding, monitoring) and Vehicle (fallback autonomy, actuator response). Mitigation: define clear Tasking and SafetyAlert interface contracts.
Low risk: Network protocol selection (UDP vs. TCP) is localized to Teleoperation module; Vehicle does not need to know protocol details.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module can contain DP-C's core logic (command encoding, network protocol, latency monitoring, mode switching). Vehicle module contains fallback autonomy and actuator response. Safety Services contains latency-triggered safety policies. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are sufficient to coordinate across modules without diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Latency can be measured end-to-end via instrumentation of Teleoperation module (command timestamp) and Vehicle module (response timestamp). Telemetry interface can carry latency metrics. SafetyAlert can report violations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation owns latency performance and monitoring. Vehicle owns actuator response latency. Safety Services owns safety interventions. Allocation is unambiguous and testable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify latency budget allocation: sensor acquisition (≤30 ms), network (≤80 ms), command execution (≤40 ms). Assign each budget to owning module.
Define SafetyAlert protocol to include latency violation messages with severity levels and recommended actions.
Specify Network Link interface requirements for latency measurement and reporting.
Document fallback autonomy trigger thresholds and mode transition procedures in Vehicle and Teleoperation interface contracts.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (sensor latency, network latency, command execution latency, latency monitoring). DP-C is sound and well-aligned with Teleoperation module. However, to ensure complete coverage and avoid gaps, each sub-FR must be decomposed to the next level. This is a normal and healthy zigzag: FR → DP → IM (current level) is complete and passes gates; now decompose sub-FRs to ensure all latency contributors are addressed.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission (uplink and downlink) shall introduce ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Remote command processing and actuator response shall introduce ≤ 40 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations to operator and safety systems

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute capacity for edge processing and local autonomy fallback.
Network conditions are measurable and predictable within agreed bounds (p95 ≤ 150 ms).
Operator can safely manage mode transitions between tele-op and autonomous fallback.
SafetyAlert interface can support real-time latency violation reporting.
Telemetry interface provides sufficient bandwidth and frequency for latency monitoring.
Network Link interface is available and measurable for latency instrumentation.
open_risks: Edge compute capacity may be insufficient; early hardware validation required.
Network variability may exceed agreed bounds; fallback autonomy must be safe under worst-case latency.
Operator confusion during mode transitions; clear HMI feedback and training required.
SafetyAlert protocol may not support latency reporting; protocol definition required.
Latency monitoring overhead may impact performance; instrumentation design required.
evidence_needed: Vehicle hardware specifications (compute capacity, actuator response time).
Network conditions characterization (latency distribution, jitter, packet loss under agreed conditions).
Fallback autonomy design and safety case (how safe is autonomous operation if tele-op fails?).
SafetyAlert protocol specification (message formats, priorities, latency requirements).
Telemetry interface specification (bandwidth, frequency, latency measurement support).
Operator training and HMI design for mode transitions.
End-to-end latency measurement methodology and test plan.
